ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for a customisable grain bowl with multiple toppings; FOODON_00002473 (grain-based food product) used as the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (grain-based mixed dish) is the closest available category for a customisable grain bowl; no exact code exists for this specific format.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for customisable grain bowl; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: whole grains (brown rice, quinoa, or similar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard grain bowl serving is approximately 350 g, comprising a base of whole grains (roughly 150 g), three toppings such as vegetables and a protein (approximately 150 g combined), and a dressing (approximately 50 g).

nutrient_source.title: Grain Bowl (3 Toppings) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Grain Bowl (3 Toppings), cr_id F08010028, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed grain bowl / brown rice analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for a customisable grain bowl with 3 toppings. GI of 55 is estimated using the University of Sydney GI Database entry for brown rice (GI ~55, low) as the primary grain base analogue, consistent with the HPB record description of a whole-grain base. Glycemic load per serving (350 g) calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (12.79 g/100g - 1.76 g/100g fibre = 11.03 g/100g net carbs; 11.03 g/100g * 3.5 serving size = 38.6 g net carbs per serving) ÷ 100 = 55 × 38.6 / 100 ≈ 21.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 229 mg/100g is borderline elevated; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 802 mg sodium, representing about 35% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request dressing on the side and choose low-sodium sauce options, as soy-based dressings are the primary sodium driver in this dish; a 350 g serving contributes approximately 802 mg sodium and should be accounted for within the daily 2,300 mg limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content (12.79 g/100g) with 1.76 g dietary fibre and a GI of approximately 55 (low, brown rice analogue) yields a manageable glycaemic response when whole grains are selected.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should choose a whole-grain base (brown rice or quinoa) over white rice to maintain a lower glycaemic index, monitor dressing sugar content, and account for the approximately 38.6 g of available carbohydrates in a standard 350 g serving within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is moderate at 5.22 g/100g with estimated saturated fat of approximately 0.85 g/100g; dietary fibre at 1.76 g/100g and whole-grain base support cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The grain bowl is broadly suitable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease due to its low saturated fat content (~0.85 g/100g), moderate dietary fibre (1.76 g/100g), and whole-grain base; however, sodium at 229 mg/100g should be managed by limiting high-sodium dressings and requesting sauce on the side.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.85 g/100g), dietary fibre (1.76 g/100g), and antioxidant-rich vegetable toppings support anti-inflammatory dietary patterns relevant to RA management.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from this dish by selecting anti-inflammatory toppings such as leafy greens, turmeric-seasoned protein, or ginger-based dressing, while avoiding nightshade vegetables (tomato, bell pepper) if symptoms are active.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A grain bowl with 3 toppings is a customisable, health-oriented mixed dish popular in Singapore's contemporary café and health-casual dining scene. It consists of a base of whole grains — typically brown rice, quinoa, or a blend — topped with three user-selected ingredients such as roasted or steamed vegetables, a lean protein (chicken, tofu, egg, or legumes), and a dressing or sauce. The dish is assembled rather than cooked as a single unit, meaning nutritional composition varies with topping and dressing choices. Per 100 g, it provides 110 kcal, 2.97 g protein, 5.22 g fat, 12.79 g carbohydrates, 1.76 g dietary fibre, 3.56 g sugar, and 229 mg sodium, with 19 mg calcium and 0.89 mg iron. The texture is varied — chewy grains, tender or crisp vegetables, and a moist dressing — and the flavour profile ranges from savoury-umami (soy-based dressings) to tangy or mildly sweet depending on topping selection. Clinically, the dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when a whole-grain base is chosen, as the GI is approximately 55 (low, brown rice analogue) and fibre moderates glucose response. Sodium at 229 mg/100g is borderline for hypertension management and dressing selection is critical. Saturated fat is low, making it broadly suitable for cardiovascular health. The dish is a common lunch or dinner option in Singapore's health-food outlets, hawker-adjacent cafés, and workplace canteens, and aligns with HPB Singapore's dietary guidelines for balanced meals.
common_user_queries: Is grain bowl good for diabetes?
Grain bowl with brown rice calories

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08010028) with a stated confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using brown rice as the closest approved analogue, which is appropriate given the whole-grain base description. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for comparable grain bowl compositions as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.85 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable mixed grain dishes (USDA FDC ID 169704, brown rice cooked, and mixed vegetable analogues) as the HPB Foodo record does not report saturated fat breakdown. This is an approximation and should be verified if a more precise HPB breakdown becomes available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this customisable grain bowl are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F08010028) as of 2026-04-16, and no sufficiently close analogue in USDA FoodData Central provides a complete panel for this specific multi-topping format. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for brown rice (GI ~55, low); the actual GI will vary depending on the specific grain base and toppings selected. Glycemic load per 350 g serving is approximately 21.2 g. 4. Allergen profile is indicative only; actual allergens depend on topping and dressing selection. 5. Dietary flags (vegetarian, vegan, halal) are indicative defaults; actual suitability depends on protein topping chosen. 6. The dish is classified as a Singaporean contemporary health-casual dish; multilingual names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are provided as functional translations of the English descriptor rather than established native-language dish names, as this is a modern customisable format without a single canonical local-language name.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 requirements.
Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 requirements.
Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for GI 55, as per University of Sydney GI Database guidelines (GI 55 is typically considered low).
Recalculated glycemic_load_per_serving to 21.2 based on net carbohydrates per 350g serving and updated gi_source notes accordingly.
